essential_question: Of everything from Lessons 1–10, what still has to be automatic before the Final?
objective: Review Unit 01's foundations — points, lines, and planes; angle types and pairs; polygons and perimeter; area; and triangle classification — through mixed cumulative practice.

agenda.title: Warm-Up — The Start of the Year

content.prompt: Unit 01 was Lessons 1–10 — the vocabulary and toolkit of the whole course. Without checking anything, what's the first idea from those ten lessons that comes to mind?

content.heading: Foundations, angles, polygons, area, triangles
bullets: Points, lines, rays, segments, and planes — named by endpoints, and how planes intersect.
Parallel lines, transversals, and the angle relationships they create.
Polygons, quadrilaterals, and perimeter; area of parallelograms, triangles, and trapezoids.
Classifying triangles by sides (scalene/isosceles/equilateral) and by angles.

content.preview: Session 2: Unit 02 — circles, solids, radicals, and the Pythagorean theorem.

success_criteria.text: I can identify points, lines, and planes and how they intersect
I can classify angles and angle pairs (complementary, supplementary) correctly
I can compute perimeter and area for basic figures
I can classify triangles by their sides

compass.note: Curriculum/Geometry/The Salas Compass/2026-W37 Year-End Final Review.md

questions.prompt: Two distinct planes that intersect meet in exactly a…
questions.explain: Two planes can't meet at just a point or a piece of a line — where they intersect is a full line.
Obtuse angles measure more than 90° and less than 180°.

questions.prompt: Two angles that sum to exactly 90° are…
questions.explain: Complementary angles sum to 90°; supplementary angles sum to 180°.

questions.prompt: Find the perimeter of a rectangle with length 12 and width 5.
Find the area of a triangle with base 10 and height 6.
questions.explain: Area of a triangle = ½ × base × height.

questions.prompt: A triangle with sides 5, 5, and 8 is classified as…
questions.explain: Two sides (the two 5s) are congruent, which makes it isosceles.

questions.prompt: Exit: which Unit 01 idea from today's mix would you want one more example of before the Final?
